title: Throwing a vase with a narrow neck
subtitle: Collaring in from a wide belly to a constricted neck at the wheel
excerpt: A wheel-thrown stoneware vase where the lower body is opened wide and the walls are then drawn upward and inward to form a narrow neck. Narrowing the neck requires a throwing stick to reach inside and support the wall from within. The finished piece is bisque fired and then glaze fired.
sourceNotes: Wheel-throwing technique for narrow-neck vessels derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Compressing and aligning clay on the spinning wheel head until it runs perfectly true with no wobble. The foundation of every thrown form.
Forming clay by hand on a spinning potter's wheel.
A clay drying stage where the piece is stiff enough to handle and trim without distorting, but still slightly damp. The correct state for foot-ring trimming.
A long wooden tool that reaches inside tall, narrow forms where the hand cannot fit, supporting the inner wall during compression.
Compressing the walls inward from the outside with both hands to narrow a cylinder or widen section without opening the form.

content.text: Throwing

a narrow-neck vase is the standard benchmark for advanced wheel work. The difficulty is in the transition: once the neck diameter drops below about 60 mm, the fingers can no longer reach inside, and the inner wall must be supported by a
throwing stick
instead. The wider belly is thrown first while there is still room for the hand; the neck is then narrowed using
collaring
passes from the outside.

attrs.content: Wear an N95 mask when trimming dry or leather-hard stoneware — trimming generates fine silica-bearing dust. Fire kilns in a well-ventilated space away from living areas.

content.text: Steps
Centre 800 g of clay and open to the belly width, roughly two-thirds of the target height.
Raise the walls in three or four pulling passes, leaving extra thickness near the top.
Collar the neck inward with both hands in repeated passes to reach 30-40 mm diameter.
Refine the neck with a throwing stick inside and compress the rim.
Cut off with a wire and trim a foot ring when leather-hard.
Centring and opening
Wedge the clay thoroughly to remove air pockets. Pat into a cone and press onto a clean, damp wheel head or bat.
Centre

at medium-high speed: brace the elbows against the body, wrap both hands around the mound, and compress inward and downward simultaneously until the clay runs true. Open the floor with both thumbs pressed together, leaving a base approximately 8 mm thick. Draw the opening outward to the desired belly width, roughly two-thirds of the total height for a tall vase.
Raising the walls

Pull up the walls in three or four lifting passes: inside finger supporting, outside hand compressing, both moving upward in sync. Keep the wheel speed steady and the pull even. After each pass, compress the rim gently between thumb and forefinger. The belly should be at its widest about one-third from the base. Leave the walls slightly thicker at the top than you would for a cylinder. The neck narrowing will use that extra clay.
Narrowing the neck

To begin the neck, reduce the wheel speed. Place both hands on the outside of the wall at the point where the neck will start, approximately 30 mm below the rim. Apply firm, even inward pressure with both hands simultaneously, sliding slowly upward in a collaring motion. Repeat two or three times, moving the collar start point slightly higher with each pass. Once the neck diameter is too narrow for fingers to enter, insert the throwing stick down through the neck opening to support the inner wall while the outside hand continues to refine the shape. The finished neck should be 30-40 mm in diameter for a 200 mm tall vase.
Finishing and cutting off

Level the rim with the needle tool if needed. Compress the outer surface with the wooden rib to refine the curve. Remove excess water from inside the form using the pottery sponge on a stick passed through the neck. Slow the wheel and draw the wire cutter under the base in one smooth pass. Slide the vase on its bat to a drying board.
Trimming when leather-hard
When the vase is
leather-hard

. Once leather-hard (typically 12-24 hours depending on conditions), invert it on the wheel head and trim a foot ring with the loop tool. Aim for a base approximately 6 mm thick and a foot ring that matches the visual weight of the form. Wear the N95 mask throughout trimming.
Variations
Add a flared lip to the narrow neck opening by gently easing the final rim outward with the tip of a finger.
Throw a wider, more globular belly for a gourd-style form using the same collaring technique for the neck.

items.symptom: Neck buckles or folds inward during collaring
items.cause: Walls too thin at the neck transition point before collaring begins
items.fix: Leave extra thickness in the upper walls during the pulling phase. Collar more gradually, using two passes at each height rather than one aggressive push

items.symptom: Throwing stick scratches the inner wall
items.cause: Stick held at wrong angle or pushed against the wall too hard
items.fix: Keep the throwing stick vertical and use it as passive support only. The outer hand does the shaping; the stick simply prevents collapse
